feature_name: Dual-mode operation (Root Complex and End Point)
description: The module supports operation as either a Root Complex (RC) or an End Point (EP). Mode can be selected at boot via PCIESSMODE[1:0] pins and overridden by software. As an end point, it can operate as a legacy or native PCIe EP. As an RC, it provides configuration and forwarding for downstream devices. This duality enables flexible system topologies using a single controller.
dependencies: Boot strap pins PCIESSMODE[1:0]; DEVSTAT (device-specific); CMD_STATUS[LTSSM_EN]
Remarks: Select mode early and reprogram only when link is disabled; plan software paths for both roles.

feature_name: PCIe link width and speed (x1/x2, Gen1/Gen2)
description: Supports a single bidirectional link up to two lanes (×2). Raw speeds of 2.5 or 5.0 Gbps per lane are supported with 8b/10b encoding. Maximum negotiated link width and speed are exposed by capability/status registers. Lane reversal and polarity inversion improve routing flexibility. Gen2 transition is software-controlled post Gen1 initialization.
evidence: Section 1.3 Features; LINK_CAP[MAX_LINK_WIDTH, MAX_LINK_SPEED]; LINK_STAT_CTRL[LINK_SPEED, NEGOTIATED_LINK_WD]; PL_GEN2[DIR_SPD]
dependencies: Common clock considerations (LINK_STAT_CTRL[COMMON_CLK_CFG]); SerDes PLL lock
Remarks: Set LN_EN and DIR_SPD appropriately; verify common-clock or non-common-clock timing before training.

feature_name: Endian conversion for inbound/outbound transactions
description: The subsystem supports system big-endian mode with programmable byte swapping. Separate modes control inbound and outbound conversion on 1-, 2-, 4-, or 8-byte boundaries. The ENDIAN register governs swap behavior when the SoC is configured big-endian. This allows interoperability with the PCIe little-endian transport while maintaining CPU endianness. Byte enables are also swapped consistently with data.
dependencies: SoC big-endian boot configuration; ENDIAN register programming
Remarks: Validate DMA descriptors and buffer layouts under chosen swap mode; test both directions.

feature_name: Outbound address translation (32 regions, 1–8 MB each)
description: Outbound transactions can be remapped from internal bus space to PCIe memory space using 32 equally-sized regions. Region size is 1, 2, 4, or 8 MB set by OB_SIZE, and each region has 32- and 64-bit base offset registers. Transactions crossing a translation boundary have unspecified behavior and must be sized accordingly. This provides flexible mapping of host memory to remote PCIe space. Software must enable each region individually.
dependencies: CMD_STATUS[OB_XLT_EN]=1; Correct OB_SIZE and region enables
Remarks: Partition memory so bursts do not straddle region edges; document region map for DMA clients.

feature_name: Inbound address translation and BAR-based mapping (4 windows)
description: Inbound requests are accepted based on BAR matches and mapped into internal Address Space 1 using four translation windows. Each window selects a BAR, a PCIe start address, and a local offset to produce the target system address. BAR0 is reserved for application/register space and cannot be remapped. An option allows using BAR1 value as the start address for translation, simplifying EP setups.
dependencies: Proper BAR programming (Type 0/1); CMD_STATUS[IB_XLT_EN]=1
Remarks: Ensure no inbound I/O is attempted; define non-overlapping windows and align to avoid partial remaps.

feature_name: BAR Mask registers and pre-enumeration control
description: BAR Mask registers allow software to program EP BAR sizes, types, and enablement prior to RC enumeration. Access to mask overlays requires DBI_CS2=1 and reads return the underlying BAR, so verification uses test writes. BAR0 mask is limited as BAR0 targets application registers by design. This mechanism tailors requested address space to system constraints.
dependencies: DBI_CS2 enable/disable sequencing; EP mode only for RC-programmed BARs
Remarks: Clear DBI_CS2 before link-up; verify mask effect by probing BAR with test patterns as shown.

feature_name: Message Signaled Interrupts (MSI) generation and reception
description: The controller supports MSI both as RC and EP. MSI addresses and data are configured in MSI capability registers; writing the configured vector value generates the interrupt. MSI events are grouped into eight banks of four with enable, status, and raw status registers. In EP mode MSI can be targeted to RC or, as a provisioning aid, to another EP’s BAR0 MSI handler. Software must clear status and write IRQ_EOI per event.
evidence: Sections 2.14.2.2, 2.14.5.2; MSI_CAP/MSI_LOW32/MSI_UP32/MSI_DATA (3.6); MSI_IRQ and MSIn_* (3.1.16, 3.1.24–3.1.55); IRQ_EOI (3.1.15)
dependencies: Configured MSI address/data; BAR0 visibility for MSI_IRQ; IRQ controller mapping
Remarks: Account for MSI/posted write reordering; clear MSIn_IRQ_STATUS and issue EOI before re-enabling.

feature_name: Legacy INTx support (EP generate, RC receive)
description: Legacy interrupts are supported with EP-side assert/deassert message generation and RC-side reception. EP uses EP_IRQ_SET/CLR/STATUS and configuration INT_PIN to select INTA–INTD. RC exposes enabled status and enables per legacy line, and EOI must be written after servicing. As per PCIe, endpoints cannot receive legacy interrupts, so only generation (EP) and reception (RC) are applicable.
dependencies: Correct INTx enable bits; system interrupt routing; EOI handling
Remarks: Beware race between deassert message and EOI; probe downstream INT status before issuing EOI.

feature_name: Interrupt event mapping (14 events) and EOI protocol
description: A total of fourteen interrupt lines are exposed to the SoC interrupt controller. These cover INTx, MSI groups, error events, and power/reset events. Each has enable, status, and raw status registers, and IRQ_EOI is used to mark end of interrupt per event number. Software must service and clear status before acknowledging to prevent retrigger. This unified map simplifies integration with the device IRQ fabric.
evidence: Section 2.14.1; Table 2-10 PCIESS Interrupt Events; IRQ_EOI (3.1.15); ERR_* and PMRST_* regs (3.1.72–3.1.79)
dependencies: Interrupt controller configuration; correct event numbers in IRQ_EOI
Remarks: Document event-to-CPU interrupt routing; group related MSI vectors to appropriate handlers.

feature_name: Device and Link Power Management (D-states, ASPM L0s/L1)
description: The controller implements PCI and PCIe power management states. Device states D0, optional D1/D2, and D3hot/cold are supported via PM capability and control/status registers. Link power management supports ASPM L0s and L1, with software or hardware entry depending on configuration. L2 is not supported; L3 implies full power-off. Power state transitions interact with link states per Table 2-11 and must be coordinated with software.
dependencies: PSC/module power domains; PME/ASPM enables; reference clock requirements
Remarks: Gate traffic and drain FIFOs before L1; avoid L2 usage; use PME where required for wake flows.

feature_name: Advanced Error Reporting (AER) and error logging
description: Advanced error reporting is implemented with uncorrectable and correctable status/mask/severity registers. The first error pointer identifies the earliest uncorrectable error, and header log registers capture the offending TLP header. Root ports expose Root Error Command/Status and Error Source ID to route and diagnose errors. Software can select severity and masking to match system policies and generate SERR/PME as needed.
evidence: Sections 2.16.2.3; PCIE_UNCERR/UN CERR_MASK/UNCERR_SVRTY (3.8.3–3.8.5); PCIE_CERR/CERR_MASK (3.8.6–3.8.7); PCIE_ACCR (3.8.8); ROOT_ERR_CMD/ROOT_ERR_ST/ERR_SRC_ID (3.8.13–3.8.15)
dependencies: Enable AER messaging via DEV_STAT_CTRL and ROOT_CTRL_CAP; error masks
Remarks: Log and clear before EOI to avoid losing subsequent events; tune severity for application tolerance.

feature_name: Baseline PCIe and PCI-compatible error handling
description: Baseline error reporting uses DEV_STAT_CTRL to enable reporting of correctable, non-fatal, and fatal errors and Unsupported Request detection. The PCI-compatible STATUS_COMMAND reflects events like Master Abort, Target Abort, and Parity Error, mapped to PCIe semantics. Root ports can retrain links and monitor link error states. This provides backward-compatible reporting for software stacks expecting legacy fields.
dependencies: Driver config of reporting enables; error class handlers
Remarks: Use PCIe-specific AER where available; keep PCI-compatible bits for OS enumeration compatibility.

feature_name: PIPE loopback (link-layer symbol loopback)
description: The controller supports PIPE loopback enabling symbol-level testing with a loopback master/slave arrangement. In RC mode, enabling LPBK_EN and initiating retrain enters loopback; in EP mode, loopback uses PL_LINK_CTRL and PL_FORCE_LINK controls. These modes do not loop back TLPs and are intended for PHY/link validation with test equipment. Exit requires clearing loopback and retraining the link.
dependencies: Link must be trained to appropriate states; coordination with partner or tester
Remarks: Use only with compliance fixtures; not suitable for functional traffic validation.

feature_name: PHY loopback (SerDes local TX-to-RX)
description: PHY loopback internally routes transmitted data back to the receiver at the PHY, enabling TLP loopback without a partner. Configuration uses SERDES_CFGx to enable TX/RX loopback and PL_FORCE_LINK to force LTSSM states. RC mode is required due to upstream-only limitations for EP. Additional constraints apply: configure before traffic, avoid BAR conflicts, and do not attempt configuration transactions in this mode.
dependencies: SerDes PLL lock; RC mode; correct LTSSM force sequence
Remarks: Set loopback prior to training; carefully select target addresses to avoid internal register hits.

feature_name: Ack/Nak latency and replay timing control
description: Ack/Nak latency and replay timers are programmable to tune link behavior. PL_ACKTIMER exposes round-trip and replay limits, while SYM_NUM provides additional modifiers. The ACK frequency can be adjusted to send ACK DLLPs after N TLPs, or disabled. These controls can optimize performance under different channel conditions. Misconfiguration can reduce throughput or increase error recovery.
dependencies: Stable link training; knowledge of channel and clocking
Remarks: Use default values first; adjust only when characterization shows benefit; verify with stress tests.

feature_name: SerDes PLL configuration and lock status
description: The integrated SerDes PLL multiplies an external differential reference clock to line rate. PCIE_SERDES_CFGPLL sets multiplication, bandwidth, sleep, and enable, and PCIE_SERDES_STS reports lock and lane status. The PCIe logic is clocked from the PHY PLL, so transactions must not start until LOCK is asserted. Reference clock quality and bandwidth settings impact jitter tolerance and eye opening.
dependencies: Device kicker mechanism to write chip-level regs; stable REFCLK; PSC domain on
Remarks: Wait >1 µs post-ENPLL and confirm LOCK before enabling LTSSM; choose LB setting per clock source.

feature_name: Transaction sizing, alignment, and strobe constraints
description: Outbound internal bus transactions are limited to 128 bytes, and misaligned starts reduce allowable size to 120 bytes. Read interleaving is avoided when transaction size does not exceed max size. Write byte enables must be contiguous (no holes) within a transfer. Adhering to these rules prevents undefined behavior and bus lock-ups. Payloads and read request sizes are capped per device capabilities.
dependencies: DMA/EDMA programming; buffer alignment
Remarks: Align buffers to 8 bytes; split large bursts at 128-byte boundaries; enforce contiguous byte strobes.

feature_name: DMA via VBUSM master/slave and EDMA examples
description: The PCIe subsystem has 128-bit VBUSM master and slave ports but no internal DMA engine. In RC mode, an SoC DMA can move data to/from remote devices via the slave port, and inbound bus mastering from PCIe uses the master port. In EP mode, outbound accesses originate from the slave port and inbound arrive on the master. Examples demonstrate EDMA setup and note that achievable throughput is about 85% of protocol data rate per lane and direction.
dependencies: EDMA controller; outbound/inbound translation enabled; bus bandwidth
Remarks: Dimension DMA aCnt/bCnt to max payload; ensure address mapping correctness before starting transfers.

feature_name: LTSSM and link state debug registers
description: DEBUG0 and DEBUG1 expose LTSSM state, TS1/TS2 reception, link-up indication, PIPE data, and control flags. Appendix A provides the encoding map from numeric state to LTSSM state names. These registers aid bring-up, training issues, and runtime diagnostics. They are read-only and update in real time. Use them to confirm loopback, reset, and training transitions.
dependencies: Accessible address space 0 application registers; stable link clocks
Remarks: Poll during bring-up to sequence operations; log state transitions for field diagnostics.

feature_name: Gen2 controls: directed speed change and de-emphasis
description: PL_GEN2 allows software to command a directed speed change to Gen2 after Gen1 link-up. It also configures TX swing and de-emphasis levels (−6 dB or −3.5 dB) for compliance and channel tuning. Proper use requires coordinating both link partners. Incorrect settings can prevent training or degrade BER. These controls enable optimal operation on marginal channels.
dependencies: Both ends must support Gen2; channel qualifies for selected deemphasis
Remarks: Apply only after verifying common-clock and equalization; revert to Gen1 if instability is detected.

feature_name: Address Space 0 windows (app regs, local/remote config, remote I/O)
description: Address Space 0 (16 KB) splits into four 4 KB windows for application registers, local configuration, remote configuration, and (RC only) remote I/O. Local and remote configuration spaces are accessed as Type 0/1 and are 32-bit access only. The remote I/O window maps to IOBASE high bits and emits PCIe I/O TLPs. None of these regions support bursts and must be treated non-cacheable.
dependencies: CFG_SETUP for bus/dev/fn/type; IOBASE programmed; 32-bit accesses only
Remarks: Check link-up before remote accesses; serialize config/I/O transactions to avoid timeouts.

feature_name: I/O TLP support restrictions (RC only, outbound)
description: Outbound I/O transactions are supported in RC mode via a dedicated 4 KB window and IOBASE register. Inbound I/O and any EP I/O accesses are not supported. Software should avoid configuring inbound I/O BARs and must not expect device response to inbound I/O cycles. This simplifies implementation while preserving needed legacy access from RC.
dependencies: RC mode; IOBASE configured; CFG_SETUP for Type 1 if needed
Remarks: Prefer memory-mapped I/O where possible; constrain to 32-bit, aligned I/O writes/reads.

feature_name: Programmable TLP filtering and error-policy masks
description: Filtering registers allow treating certain malformed or out-of-policy TLPs as Unsupported Requests or passing them up. Options include masking completion attribute/length/tag/function checks, treating out-of-BAR and poisoned TLPs as UR or supported, and controlling vendor message drop. These controls help harden against non-compliant devices or during bring-up. Misuse can hide real protocol violations.
dependencies: Consistent policy with AER settings; careful enable of exceptions
Remarks: Use restrictive defaults; open filters only during controlled debug; log exceptions.

feature_name: MSI reception in EP mode (device-to-device or RC-to-EP)
description: Although PCIe specs target MSI to RC, the module allows MSI writes to EP’s BAR0 MSI_IRQ to signal local events. MSIn group registers enable, status, and clear per 32 vectors with 4-per-event grouping. This can be used for EP–EP messaging in KeyStone-based systems. Software must clear status and signal EOI to the SoC interrupt controller accordingly.
dependencies: BAR0 exposure; properly routed PCIe write; IRQ fabric mapping
Remarks: Scope to trusted peers; avoid flooding by batching interrupt vectors; verify ordering hazards.

feature_name: Reset and hot-reset sequencing
description: Software can command a downstream hot reset using RSTCMD[INIT_RST], with FLUSH_N indicating pending transactions. Hardware and software resets differ in scope; sticky bits are retained across soft reset. After reset, software must wait at least 100 ms before issuing transactions and up to 1.5 s for device responsiveness. Power-domain cycling resets the block and requires reinitialization and link training.
evidence: Sections 2.13.2, 2.13.1; RSTCMD[INIT_RST, FLUSH_N] (3.1.7); 2.13.3 Power Domain Considerations
dependencies: PSC power/clock gating; LTSSM enable after reset delays
Remarks: Quiesce traffic and check FLUSH_N before reset; follow timing guidelines to avoid false timeouts.

feature_name: Relaxed ordering and no-snoop attributes
description: Global control of relaxed ordering and no-snoop attributes for all outgoing TLPs is provided. These attributes can improve performance but may affect ordering semantics and cache coherency. The RC can also disable relaxed ordering requests via DEV_STAT_CTRL and TLPCFG. Tuning must respect system memory ordering guarantees. Incorrect use can break barriers or DMA sequencing.
evidence: TLPCFG[RELAXED, NO_SNOOP] (3.1.6); DEV_STAT_CTRL[RELAXED, NO_SNOOP] (3.7.4); Section 2.14.5 note
dependencies: System interconnect rules; device and OS expectations
Remarks: Default to conservative (disabled) and enable only for known-safe paths; audit drivers using RO/NS.

feature_name: Application and general-purpose registers
description: Application registers provide control for initialization, resets, power, endian, priorities, diagnostic error injection, and general-purpose use. GPR0–GPR3 are available for firmware/driver communication. DIAG_CTRL supports single-packet LCRC/ECRC inversion for test. These registers form the primary software control plane for the PCIeSS.
dependencies: Address Space 0 access; 32-bit access width only
Remarks: Keep GP registers’ usage documented; restrict DIAG error injection to controlled tests.

feature_name: TLP attribute and priority configuration
description: The TLP attribute register sets global relaxed-ordering and no-snoop attributes. PRIORITY allows assigning transaction priority and privilege ID for inbound master requests. These settings help balance traffic and integrate with SoC QoS. Improper priority assignment can starve critical flows or violate privilege separation.
dependencies: QoS policy; interconnect arbitration behavior
Remarks: Start with default priorities; change only with system-level validation; use MST_PRIV carefully.

feature_name: Configuration access engine (local/remote)
description: CFG_SETUP selects bus, device, function, and Type 0/1 for outbound configuration requests. Local configuration space is mapped in AS0 for direct access and mirrors standard PCIe capabilities and extended capabilities. This enables enumeration as RC and provisioning of EP read-only fields before enumeration. All config-space accesses are 32-bit and non-burst.
dependencies: RC/EP mode selection; ensure LTSSM up for remote config
Remarks: Disable APP_RETRY_EN only after initialization; serialize config cycles; avoid during link transitions.
